figures.caption: Figure 1. (a) Single label-specific prototypes ignore domain variation, leading to semantic conflict when features from different domains are forced to align to general prototype. (b) Our domainspecific prototypes disentangle the feature space along both label semantics (vertical axis) and domain characteristics (horizontal axis) and enabling the better semantic alignment to the appropriate prototype.
Table 1. Comparison on DomainNet and Office-10 datasets. “Avg” denotes the mean accuracy across all domains, and ∆ indicates improvement over the FedAvg baseline.
Table 2. Comparison on the PACS dataset. “Avg” denotes the mean accuracy across all domains, and ∆ indicates improvement over the FedAvg baseline.
Figure 4. t-SNE visualization of learned features on the DomainNet and PACS benchmarks. In each subfigure, the left panel shows FedProto and the right panel shows our method.
Figure 3. Visualization of training curves of average test accuracy on three datasets under the domain shift setting.

Figure 2. Illustration of FedDAP. (1) Clients first compute local prototypes p(c,d) m based on Eq. 3 and then upload it to server. (2) The central server performs the cosine similarity-weighted fusion mechanism for the local prototypes by calculating the attention score in Eq. 4 for each row of class-domain pair prototypes. Then we perform the Domain-Specific Global Prototype aggregation using Eq. 5. (3) Clients download the global prototypes Pc,d from the server and perform the local training process with the dual prototype alignment strategy with LDPA in Eq. 6 and LCPCL in Eq. 8.
Figure 5. Analysis of FedDAP across all datasets with different hyper-parameters.
Table 8. Comparison on DomainNet and Office-10 datasets under equal client allocation. “Avg” denotes the mean accuracy across all domains, and ∆ indicates improvement over the FedAvg baseline.)
Table 9. Comparison on the PACS dataset under equal client allocation. “Avg” denotes the mean accuracy across all domains, and ∆ indicates improvement over the FedAvg baseline.
